web.title: Swimming Pool Resurfacing vs Replastering Explained
web.description: While replastering involves just replacing the plaster, resurfacing involves replacing the plaster with any modern material, such as fibreglass, tiles, or ...
web.markdown:
It is nice having a pool in your backyard. Having a ready relief from the hot weather or a cool venue for a nice house party is not a luxury many have. However, most pool owners stop at having a pool constructed. They don’t go into the details of how it should be made, what materials to use, or how to maintain it properly and leave it to the “experts.”

While we at Fibrestyle Pool Resurfacing pride ourselves on being experts, we understand why customers don’t have the time to learn these things. We are willing to share everything we know about prolonging the life of your precious investment. The differences between these terms are essential to appreciate dowel costs, especially with swimming pool resurfacing, to prolong the life of any investment.

That’s right! Read on to learn the difference between replastering and resurfacing and discover which one you need.

Let’s get started!

## **What Is Replastering?**

Replastering means repairing or replacing the interior plaster of a swimming pool. In most cases, replastering is needed when the original plaster is already aged, damaged, or cracked because of chemical exposure.

#### **How Does Replastering Work?**

1. **Draining the Pool** – First, the pool is drained to give access to the inside surface.
2. **Preparing the Surface** – The damaged plaster is chipped away or sanded down, creating a clean, rough surface that will accept the new layer better.
3. **Applying New Plaster**—The entire pool interior is covered with a fresh layer of plaster, mostly consisting of a mixture of cement, sand, and water.
4. **Curing**—The new plaster must be properly cured, which generally takes a few days. During this process, it hardens and sets.

#### **When Should You Replaster?**

If your existing plaster is heavily worn, stained, or cracked, replastering is highly recommended. Poor pool plaster naturally creates rough areas that are displeasing to the eye and can cause discomfort when swimming.

## **What Is Pool Resurfacing?**

While replastering involves just replacing the plaster, resurfacing involves replacing the plaster with any modern material, such as fibreglass, tiles, or aggregate finishes, depending on your taste and budget.

#### **Steps Involved in Resurfacing a Pool**

1. **Assessment and Preparation** – Similar to replastering, professionals drain the pool and inspect the existing surface for damage.
2. **Surface Removal** – Professionals may remove or repair old layers of plaster, fibreglass, or other materials.
3. **New Surface Application**—Professionals install a new interior layer based on the chosen material. This could be plaster, a fibreglass coating, or a pebblecrete finish.
4. **Polishing and Sealing** – Professionals polish or seal the new surface to enhance its durability and aesthetic appeal.

Check out how we handle resurfacing pools [here](https://www.fibrestyle.com.au/service/pool-resurfacing/).

### **Replastering and Resurfacing: Key Differences**

To better understand how these processes differ, let’s compare them across various factors.

|     |     |     |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Factor** | **Replastering** | **Resurfacing** |
| **Definition** | Applying a new layer of plaster to the pool interior. | Applying a new interior finish (plaster, fibreglass, tiles, etc.). |
| **Material Options** | Typically limited to plaster materials. | Offers multiple options, including fibreglass and pebblecrete. |
| **Scope of Work** | Focused solely on the plaster surface. | Involves complete interior surface renewal with flexible material choices. |
| **Durability** | Less durable if plaster is the only material used. | Can last longer depending on material selection. |
| **Cost** | Generally, it is more affordable upfront. | Costs can vary based on material but may offer better long-term savings. |
| **Aesthetic Impact** | Maintains the pool’s original look. | Enhances aesthetics with modern designs and finishes. |

![](<Base64-Image-Removed>)

### **Benefits of Swimming Pool Resurfacing**

Swimming pool resurfacing has several benefits, including:

#### **1\. Enhanced Durability**

Resurfacing a swimming pool can double its lifespan by replacing old, weak materials with newer, stronger ones. Fibreglass is a good example, as it is more resistant to cracks and stains.

#### **2\. Improved Aesthetics**

Resurfacing materials come in a wide variety of colours and styles. You could select the finish that fits your desired look, from a luxurious mosaic to a smooth pebblecrete texture.

#### **3\. Reduced Maintenance**

New resurfacing materials like fibreglass require minimal to no maintenance and lesser chances of leakage and deterioration. Surfaces made from fibreglass are devoid of pores and are, therefore, more resistant to algae. They are also easier to clean.

### **Popular Swimming Pool Resurfacing Materials**

When deciding on the best option for swimming pool resurfacing, consider the advantages of the following materials:

#### **1\. Plaster**

●       Traditional and cost-effective.

●       Requires periodic maintenance, usually every 7 to 10 years.

#### **2\. Fibreglass**

●       Highly durable and resistant to cracking.

●       Low maintenance due to its smooth, non-porous surface.

#### **3\. Pebblecrete (Pebble Finish)**

●       Provides a textured, natural appearance.

●       Strong and long-lasting, though slightly more expensive than plaster.

#### **4\. Tiles**

●       Extremely durable and customisable with various designs and colours.

●       One of the most expensive resurfacing options but offers premium aesthetics.

For other choices, you can check out our comprehensive guide on resurfacing a pool.

### **When Should You Choose Pool Resurfacing?**

There can be several reasons why you should consider swimming pool resurfacing. Here are a few possible ones:

●       The entire pool interior needs an upgrade, not just a repair.

●       You want to enhance durability and improve aesthetics.

●       You prefer a modern material, such as fibreglass or pebblecrete, for long-term performance.

●       You see signs of deterioration in your pool, such as visible cracks or chips, discolouration, rough patches, and leaks.

If you are considering addressing one of these aesthetic and functional problems, a swimming pool resurfacing might be the answer.

Learn more about our services for [**Swimming pool resurfacing**](https://www.fibrestyle.com.au/service/pool-resurfacing/).

### **Cost Considerations**

Pool maintenance costs depend on quite a range of factors, such as size, materials, and complexity of work. Replastering is the more affordable of the two. Labour and the actual plaster material account for most of the cost. On the other hand, the cost of swimming pool resurfacing depends on the type of material used. However, although resurfacing has a relatively high initial cost, one can expect to save money in the long run because of the increased durability and reduced need for maintenance.

**So, Which Option Is Best for You?**

Deciding between replastering and resurfacing depends on your pool’s condition, budget, and aesthetic goals. If you are only looking to repair your pool’s leaky plaster, replastering might do the trick. However, if you want to overhaul and upgrade your pool’s appearance and performance, swimming pool resurfacing is the best investment.

### **Final Thoughts**

We hope that we have clarified a few things about these two common processes. You are making the right choice, learning more, and being more proactive about maintaining one of your important investments.

When you’ve decided, head on to our site. Fiberstyle guarantees quality service with our team of experts who can cater to any pool type. Call us, and together, we’ll work on the materials most suitable for your budget and design preferences.

Are you ready to make your pool shine again and work like new? Visit our Swimming pool resurfacing page to begin!

web.description: Explore the pros and cons of pool resurfacing versus replastering to determine the best solution for your pool maintenance needs.
web.markdown:
[Skip to main content](https://ecostylepools.com.au/blog/pool-resurfacing-vs-replastering/#brx-content) [Skip to footer](https://ecostylepools.com.au/blog/pool-resurfacing-vs-replastering/#brx-footer)

![Pool Resurfacing Verus Replastering](https://ecostylepools.com.au/wp-content/uploads/pool-resurfacing-verus-replastering-1536x1024.jpg.webp)

Grant Osborneon

02/05/2024

# Pool Resurfacing vs Replastering

Pool resurfacing and replastering might seem like nuanced distinctions in the broader context of pool maintenance, yet they hold their ground with significant differences and benefits. Resurfacing, known for its versatility and durability, offers a fresh lease on life for pools showing signs of age or wear. It introduces a new layer over the existing surface, often incorporating materials that elevate the pool’s aesthetic and functional longevity.

On the flip side, replastering, while seemingly similar, focuses on renewing the plaster coating of a pool. This method is particularly beneficial for addressing cosmetic issues like staining or minor cracks, essentially giving the pool a smoother, newer look. Both approaches, though serving the same end—enhancing a pool’s condition—chart their courses through different means.

Each method—resurfacing and replastering—brings its unique set of advantages, materials, and techniques to the table. Resurfacing can involve the use of aggregate finishes or fiberglass, offering a robust solution against wear and tear. Replastering, however, traditionally uses a mixture of cement and sand, providing a classic finish that many pool owners admire.

As we delve deeper, the distinctions between [pool resurfacing](https://ecostylepools.com.au/) and replastering become clearer, from the specific materials used to the expected lifespan and maintenance requirements. This exploration will not only shed light on the technicalities and processes involved in each method but also guide pool owners in making informed decisions based on their pool’s condition, budget, and desired outcome.

## What is pool resurfacing?

Pool resurfacing is a **comprehensive process** aimed at revitalizing the overall appearance and functionality of a swimming pool. This method involves applying a _new layer_ over the existing surface of the pool, effectively covering any signs of aging, wear, or damage. The resurfacing process can utilize various materials, including **plaster**, **aggregate finishes**, **fiberglass**, or **pebble-based products**, each offering unique benefits in terms of durability, aesthetics, and maintenance requirements.

By selecting the appropriate resurfacing material, pool owners can not only **extend the lifespan** of their pool but also **enhance its visual appeal** and **resistance to future wear**. This procedure is essential for maintaining a pool’s **structural integrity** and ensuring the **safety and enjoyment** of its users.

## What is pool replastering?

Pool replastering is a _targeted method_ designed to renew the interior surface of a swimming pool. This process specifically addresses the **plaster coating**, which can deteriorate over time due to chemical imbalances, weather exposure, or general wear and tear. By removing the old, damaged plaster and applying a _fresh layer_, replastering restores the pool’s smooth texture and bright appearance.

This technique is particularly effective for correcting **cosmetic flaws** such as stains, discolorations, or minor cracks, thereby improving both the pool’s aesthetics and its **protective barrier** against leaks. Replastering is a **crucial maintenance step** for prolonging the life of a pool, ensuring a clean, inviting look that enhances the swimming experience.

## Why pools need resurfacing or replastering?

Swimming pools require resurfacing or replastering primarily due to the inevitable **wear and tear** they experience over time. Factors such as **chemical erosion**, **weather conditions**, and **physical use** contribute to the degradation of a pool’s surface. This deterioration can manifest as cracks, stains, or a rough texture, which not only diminishes the pool’s aesthetic appeal but can also compromise its **structural integrity** and **safety**.

Additionally, an aging surface may lead to water leakage, further exacerbating the problem. Resurfacing or replastering a pool restores its appearance and functionality, providing a _smoother, more durable surface_ that enhances the swimming experience. Moreover, these maintenance procedures can prevent minor issues from escalating into costly repairs, ensuring the pool remains a source of enjoyment for years to come.

## Differences between resurfacing and replastering

While both resurfacing and [pool replastering](https://ecostylepools.com.au/services/pool-replastering/) are essential maintenance processes for swimming pools, they differ significantly in scope, materials, and outcomes. **Resurfacing** is a more extensive procedure that involves covering the entire pool surface with a _new layer_, which can be made of a variety of materials such as **plaster**, **aggregate finishes**, or **fiberglass**, offering enhanced durability and aesthetic options. This method is suitable for addressing both structural issues and surface imperfections.

**Replastering**, on the other hand, specifically targets the pool’s **plaster layer**, focusing on renewing its _smooth and clean appearance_. It is particularly effective for fixing **cosmetic damages** like stains or minor cracks. The choice between resurfacing and replastering depends on the pool’s condition, the owner’s budget, and the desired longevity of the solution.

Understanding these differences is crucial for making an informed decision that aligns with the pool’s maintenance needs and the owner’s expectations.

### Materials used in each method

**Resurfacing** employs a variety of materials, including **plaster**, **aggregate finishes**, and **fiberglass**. Each offers distinct advantages, from superior durability to a wide range of aesthetic choices. In contrast, **replastering** focuses on applying a new layer of **plaster**, aimed at refurbishing the pool’s smooth surface and visual appeal.

### Aggregate finishes for resurfacing

Aggregate finishes combine plaster with elements like pebbles, quartz, or glass beads, yielding a textured, visually captivating surface. This method not only elevates the pool’s aesthetics but also enhances its resilience against chemical and physical degradation.

### Plaster finishes for replastering

Plaster finishes, utilized in replastering, are composed of cement, sand, and water. This blend produces a sleek, traditional appearance, preferred for its straightforwardness and efficacy in reviving the pool’s original allure.

### Process comparison

The **resurfacing process** encompasses a broader scope, typically involving the removal of the existing surface and the application of new material, addressing both aesthetic and structural issues. **Replastering** is more direct, concentrating on laying down a fresh plaster layer to improve the pool’s cosmetic look.

### Steps in the resurfacing process

1. **Removal** of the old surface.
2. **Repair** of any structural damage.
3. **Application** of the new surface material.
4. **Finishing touches** and curing.

### Steps in the replastering process

1. **Draining** the pool.
2. **Removing** loose or damaged plaster.
3. **Applying** the new plaster layer.
4. **Refilling** the pool and balancing water chemistry.

### Cost implications

#### Initial costs

**Replastering** generally has higher initial costs due to the extensive labor and variety of materials used. **Resurfacing**, being less labor-intensive and requiring fewer materials, tends to be more affordable initially.

#### Long-term costs

The durability of resurfacing can lead to lower long-term costs, thanks to its longevity and reduced need for repairs. Conversely, replastering, while less expensive upfront, may necessitate more frequent maintenance over time.

### Durability and lifespan

#### Expected lifespan of resurfaced pools

Depending on the materials chosen, resurfaced pools can have a significantly extended lifespan, often **10 to 20 years**, benefiting from the durable nature of aggregate or fiberglass finishes.

#### Expected lifespan of replastered pools

Replastered pools generally have a shorter lifespan, typically **5 to 10 years**, due to plaster’s inherent limitations in withstanding wear and tear.

## Choosing resurfacing or replastering

Deciding whether to opt for **resurfacing** or **replastering** hinges on several critical factors, including the **current condition** of the pool, **budget constraints**, and **long-term goals** for the pool’s use and appearance. If the pool exhibits structural issues or significant surface degradation, **resurfacing** may be the more appropriate choice, offering a comprehensive solution that addresses both _aesthetic and functional needs_. For pools with mainly cosmetic damage, such as surface stains or minor cracks, **replastering** could provide a cost-effective way to restore the pool’s beauty without the need for more extensive repairs.

Budget considerations also play a crucial role; while resurfacing might entail a higher upfront cost, its durability could lead to savings in the long run. Conversely, replastering offers immediate cost benefits but may require more frequent touch-ups. Ultimately, assessing the pool’s specific needs and weighing the pros and cons of each method will guide pool owners toward making an informed decision that aligns with their preferences and financial considerations.

### Assessing pool condition

**Evaluating the condition** of your pool is the first step in determining whether resurfacing or replastering is necessary. Look for signs of wear such as cracks, stains, or peeling surfaces, which indicate the pool’s surface has been compromised and requires attention.

### Signs your pool needs resurfacing

If you notice **extensive surface damage**, including large cracks, leaks, or areas where the underlying structure is visible, it’s a clear sign your pool needs resurfacing. These symptoms suggest that the pool’s integrity might be at risk, necessitating a comprehensive approach to restoration.

### Signs your pool needs replastering

**Minor issues** like surface stains, small cracks, or a rough texture are indicative of a pool that could benefit from replastering. This process can address cosmetic concerns, improving the pool’s appearance without extensive structural repairs.

### Budget considerations

When deciding between resurfacing and replastering, it’s crucial to consider your **budget**. Resurfacing, while more costly upfront, may offer better value in the long run due to its durability. Replastering is less expensive but might require more frequent maintenance.

### Cost-effective options for tight budgets

For those with tight budgets, replastering offers a **cost-effective solution** to refresh the pool’s appearance. Additionally, exploring different materials and contractors can help find a balance between quality and affordability.

### Value for money in the long term

Investing in resurfacing can provide **greater long-term value**, as it typically extends the pool’s lifespan and reduces the need for future repairs. This option can be more cost-efficient over time, despite the higher initial investment.

### Long-term maintenance and care

**Maintaining the condition** of your pool after resurfacing or replastering is essential for prolonging its lifespan and ensuring it remains safe and enjoyable for use.

### Maintenance needs post-resurfacing

After resurfacing, regular maintenance includes **monitoring chemical levels**, **cleaning the pool regularly**, and **inspecting the surface** for signs of wear. These practices help preserve the new surface and prevent future damage.

### Maintenance needs post-replastering

Post-replastering care is similar, focusing on **maintaining proper water chemistry**, **cleaning**, and **conducting periodic checks** for any signs of deterioration. Promptly addressing minor issues can prevent them from escalating, safeguarding the plaster’s integrity.

## Steps in pool resurfacing

Pool resurfacing is a detailed process that rejuvenates the pool’s surface, enhancing both its appearance and durability. The procedure begins with **draining the pool** to access the surface. Following this, the **old surface material is removed**, often involving chipping away plaster or sandblasting to ensure a clean base.

Any **cracks or structural damages** are then **repaired** to prevent future issues. After repairs, the **new surface material**—which can range from plaster to aggregate finishes—is **applied**. The final step involves **allowing the new surface to cure**, a critical phase that ensures the material sets properly for long-lasting results.

This comprehensive approach not only addresses aesthetic concerns but also extends the pool’s lifespan, making it a valuable investment for pool owners.

### Preparing the pool

The initial step in pool resurfacing involves thoroughly **draining the pool** to expose the surface. This is followed by a meticulous cleaning process to remove debris, algae, or any contaminants. The existing surface is then evaluated for damage, with any cracks or imperfections being **repaired** to ensure a smooth base for the new surface.

This preparation phase is crucial for the success of the resurfacing process, as it directly impacts the adherence and longevity of the new material.

### Applying the new surface

Once the pool is prepared, the **new surface material** is applied. This can vary from plaster, pebble, quartz, to fiberglass, depending on the chosen finish. The application must be done carefully and methodically to ensure an even coat that fully covers the pool’s interior.

This step requires precision and skill, as the quality of application significantly influences the final appearance and durability of the resurfacing project.

### Finishing touches and curing

After the new surface material is applied, **finishing touches** are added, which may include texturing or adding decorative elements. The pool then enters the **curing phase**, a critical period where the material needs to set and harden. During this time, the pool remains drained, and specific conditions may be maintained to facilitate optimal curing.

Once cured, the pool is refilled, and chemical levels are balanced to ensure the water is safe for swimming. This final stage completes the resurfacing process, leaving the pool refreshed and ready for enjoyment.

## Steps in pool replastering

Pool replastering is a focused process aimed at restoring the pool’s plaster surface to its original smoothness and aesthetic appeal. The procedure starts with **draining the pool**, providing access to the plaster surface. Following this, the old plaster is **inspected for damage**, and any loose or deteriorating sections are **removed**.

The surface is then **cleaned and prepped** for the new plaster application, ensuring a solid bond between the old surface and the new plaster. The **new plaster layer** is carefully **applied**, smoothed, and **leveled** to achieve a uniform finish. After the plaster application, the pool undergoes a **curing period**, where the plaster must set and harden.

Finally, the pool is **refilled**, and the water chemistry is **balanced** to ensure a safe and inviting swimming environment. This rejuvenation process not only enhances the visual appeal of the pool but also extends its lifespan, making replastering a valuable maintenance task.

### Draining and cleaning the pool

The replastering process commences with **draining the pool** to empty it of water, providing clear access to the plaster surface. This step is crucial for identifying any areas that need repair or special attention. Following drainage, the pool is **thoroughly cleaned** to remove debris, algae, and any other contaminants that could interfere with the adhesion of the new plaster.

This cleaning ensures a pristine base, which is essential for the successful application of new plaster.

### Applying new plaster

With the pool drained and cleaned, the next step is the **application of new plaster**. This involves spreading a fresh layer of plaster over the pool’s surface. The plaster must be **evenly applied** and **smoothed out** to ensure a uniform finish free of bumps or irregularities.

This process requires precision and expertise, as the quality of the application directly affects the final appearance and durability of the pool.

### Refilling and balancing water chemistry

Once the new plaster has been applied and has had sufficient time to **cure**, the final step is to **refill the pool** with water. After refilling, it’s imperative to **balance the water chemistry**, adjusting the pH, chlorine, and other chemical levels to ensure the water is safe for swimming. This step also helps protect the new plaster from premature wear and maintains the pool’s overall health and longevity.

## Risks and challenges

Embarking on pool resurfacing or replastering comes with its set of risks and challenges that pool owners must navigate carefully. One of the primary concerns is the **potential for incorrect application**, which can lead to uneven surfaces, poor adhesion, and ultimately, premature deterioration of the pool surface. Additionally, there’s the challenge of ensuring **compatibility with the existing pool structure**; using materials that don’t mesh well with the pool’s base can result in structural issues or the need for frequent repairs.

For replastering specifically, there’s the risk of **delamination**, where the new plaster fails to bond correctly with the underlying layer, causing it to peel or flake off. Ensuring a **smooth finish** is also crucial, as any imperfections can become breeding grounds for algae and bacteria, compromising the pool’s hygiene. Successfully overcoming these hurdles requires meticulous planning, expertise, and attention to detail, underscoring the importance of engaging with experienced professionals for such projects.

### For resurfacing

#### Potential for incorrect application

In pool resurfacing, a significant risk involves the **incorrect application** of materials, which can lead to a host of issues such as uneven surfaces, poor material adhesion, and a reduced lifespan of the pool surface. Ensuring the proper technique and adherence to manufacturer guidelines is crucial to mitigate this risk.

#### Compatibility with existing pool structure

Another challenge in resurfacing is ensuring the **compatibility** of the new surface materials with the existing pool structure. Materials that do not complement the underlying structure can cause problems, including cracking and structural weakness, necessitating careful selection and expert advice.

### For replastering

#### Risk of delamination

In the replastering process, **delamination** poses a significant risk. This occurs when the new plaster layer fails to properly bond with the old surface, leading to peeling or flaking. Proper surface preparation and the use of compatible materials are key to preventing delamination.

#### Ensuring a smooth finish

Achieving a **smooth finish** is essential in replastering to prevent the growth of algae and bacteria in surface imperfections. This requires meticulous application and smoothing techniques to ensure the plaster is evenly distributed and free of bumps or ridges.

## Maintaining your pool post-treatment

After undergoing resurfacing or replastering, maintaining your pool is crucial to ensure its longevity and to keep it looking its best. Regular **cleaning practices** such as skimming, vacuuming, and brushing the pool walls and floor are essential to prevent algae growth and remove debris. **Chemical balance management** plays a critical role in maintaining water quality; regular pH testing and adjustments, along with chlorine level management, are necessary to keep the water safe and clear.

Additionally, **periodic inspections** for cracks, wear, and equipment functionality are vital to catch and address any issues early on. By adhering to these maintenance routines, pool owners can enjoy a clean, healthy, and inviting swimming environment while maximizing the lifespan of their pool’s new surface.

### Regular cleaning practices

Maintaining a pool post-treatment involves **regular cleaning practices** that are crucial for preventing algae buildup and ensuring the water remains inviting. This includes removing leaves and debris, which can accumulate and affect water quality.

### Skimming and vacuuming

**Skimming the surface** daily and **vacuuming** the pool at least once a week are essential tasks. They help remove debris and dirt that can cloud the water and settle on the pool bottom, potentially staining the new surface.

### Brushing pool walls and floor

**Brushing the pool walls and floor** regularly prevents algae growth and removes any buildup that vacuuming alone might not catch. This is particularly important for textured surfaces where debris can easily hide.

### Chemical balance management

**Chemical balance management** is vital for keeping the pool water safe and extending the lifespan of the pool surface. Imbalanced water can lead to corrosion, scaling, and discomfort for swimmers.

### Regular pH testing

**Regular pH testing** ensures the water remains within the ideal range of 7.2 to 7.8. Water that’s too acidic or too alkaline can damage the pool surface and affect swimmer comfort.

### Adjusting chlorine levels

**Adjusting chlorine levels** is necessary to keep the water sanitized and clear of harmful bacteria and viruses. Chlorine levels should be maintained between 1 and 3 ppm for optimal effectiveness.

### Periodic inspections

**Periodic inspections** of the pool and its equipment can catch minor issues before they become major problems. This includes checking for leaks, ensuring pumps and filters are functioning correctly, and inspecting the pool surface for signs of wear.

### Checking for cracks and wear

Regularly **checking for cracks and wear** in the pool surface can help identify areas that may need attention or repair, preventing water leakage and further damage to the pool structure.

### Professional inspections for structural integrity

Scheduling **professional inspections** for structural integrity, especially after major treatments like resurfacing or replastering, ensures that the pool remains safe for use. Professionals can identify issues that may not be visible to the naked eye, offering peace of mind and safeguarding against future complications.

## Conclusion: Making the informed choice

Deciding between pool resurfacing and replastering requires careful consideration of various factors, including the pool’s current condition, budget constraints, and long-term maintenance expectations. Understanding the differences in materials, processes, and outcomes associated with each method is crucial for making an informed decision. Resurfacing offers a comprehensive solution for pools with structural and surface wear, providing a range of materials that cater to durability and aesthetic preferences.

Replastering, on the other hand, is ideal for addressing cosmetic issues, offering a cost-effective way to enhance the pool’s appearance. Regardless of the choice, regular maintenance post-treatment is essential for preserving the pool’s integrity and ensuring it remains a source of enjoyment for years to come. Engaging with experienced professionals and considering the long-term implications of each option will guide pool owners towards the best decision for their specific situation, ensuring a beautiful and durable pool surface.

![](https://ecostylepools.com.au/wp-content/uploads/Grant_Osborne_owner.jpg.webp)

### Grant Osborne

Grant Osborne, owner of Eco Style Pool Renovations and [Blue Stone Pools](https://bluestonepools.com.au/) has been working the pool building and maintenance industry for the over 30 years, bringing a wealth of industry knowledge to the hundreds of projects he has delivered.

Our experienced team will work with you to find the best solution for your pool, ensuring it remains a beautiful and enjoyable part of your home for years to come. # Pool Resurfacing Vs. Replastering: Which Is the Better Option?

- [Home](https://www.aquaguard5000.com/)
- News & Events
- Pool Resurfacing Vs. Replastering: Which Is the Better Option?

#### Table of Contents

1. [What Is Pool Resurfacing?](https://www.aquaguard5000.com/blog/pool-resurfacing-vs-replastering/#elementor-toc__heading-anchor-0)

2. [What Is Pool Replastering?](https://www.aquaguard5000.com/blog/pool-resurfacing-vs-replastering/#elementor-toc__heading-anchor-1)

3. [Which Is Better: Resurfacing or Replastering?](https://www.aquaguard5000.com/blog/pool-resurfacing-vs-replastering/#elementor-toc__heading-anchor-2)

4. [Quality Pool Finishing Products](https://www.aquaguard5000.com/blog/pool-resurfacing-vs-replastering/#elementor-toc__heading-anchor-3)


![pool resurfacing vs replastering](https://www.aquaguard5000.com/wp-content/uploads/2023/08/pool-resurfacing-vs-replastering.jpg)

Adding a pool to your home is a great way to boost property values and give your family a fun recreational activity. Resurfacing and replastering are two methods that can keep your pool looking in the best condition, and the team at AquaGuard 500 is here to talk to pool owners about the differences between pool resurfacing vs. replastering.

Contact us next time you search for “ [**epoxy pool paint near me**](https://www.aquaguard5000.com/epoxy-pool-paint-aquaguard-5000/) **,**” and let our professionals answer all your questions.

## What Is Pool Resurfacing?

Pools use several different types of surface finishes, like plaster, vinyl, quarts, and marcite. Resurfacing is the process of removing this top layer and reapplying the finish to the pool surface. Resurfacing is basically a facelift for your pool surface.

Contractors resurface pools by hydro blasting away the existing layer and sandblasting the underlying surface to smooth it and prepare it for the new finish. After applying the new surface, you can use your pool in about 5 to 14 days.

## What Is Pool Replastering?

Remodeling projects for pool replastering consists of completely removes the bottom plaster layer of your pool and replaces it with a new plaster surface. Replastering a pool is a more involved process than resurfacing because it replaces the entire bottom layer of the pool, not just the surface.

Plaster is a good material for pool shells for many reasons. Unlike concrete or gunite, plaster is not porous, so it won’t slowly absorb water over time. Plaster can last upwards of 20 years or more.

## Which Is Better: Resurfacing or Replastering?

When it comes to pool resurfacing vs. replastering, the right answer depends on your specific [**pool maintenance**](https://www.nitt.edu/home/students/facilitiesnservices/sportscenter/swimmingpool/SwimmingPoolMaintenance.pdf) needs.

Both resurfacing and replastering can repair damage to your pool shell and increase its usable lifespan. Resurfacing is the more cost-effective option and takes less time than replastering. However, replastering completely replaces the bottom of the pool, making it stronger and saving you money in the long run.

Replastering is also better for durability since you are removing all of the pool and replacing it with brand new materials, not just the top later. Replastering can restore an old pool to like-new condition. Both resurfacing and replastering can protect your filter system by preventing debris from the pool shell from breaking off and clogging the drains.

If your pool is over 25 years old and has noticeable cracks in the shell, you should consider replastering. Replastering will help you get the most out of old swimming pools by replacing the bottom layer, not just its top surface.

If your pool is less than 25 years old and has some discoloration or staining, resurfacing might be the better option. In general, resurfacing can fix smaller pool surface issues, while replastering is better for more significant problems.

## Quality Pool Finishing Products

Read our blog to learn more about the benefits of **fiberglass pool resurfacing**. If you would like to discuss pool renovation or pool resurfacing vs. replastering for pools, contact AquaGuard 500 online or call today at **[(772) 232-7243](tel:+17722327243)**to speak to a company representative!

#### Debbie Hardy

CFO of Aquaguard and a passionate team member who manages all financial duties. Dedicated to enhancing value for customers and our dealer network.
